title: Cat Straining to Urinate: When Frequent Litter Box Trips Become an Emergency
meta_desc: If your cat is frequently visiting the litter box without success, they may have a life-threatening blockage. Learn the signs and when to seek care.
content_html:
What is happening to your cat?

When a cat repeatedly visits the litter box but produces little to no urine, it is often a sign of Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD) or a urethral obstruction. This occurs when the tube that carries urine out of the body becomes blocked by crystals, stones, or inflammatory debris. It is a distressing and painful condition where the bladder fills to capacity but cannot empty, causing extreme discomfort.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

This is a critical medical emergency. If your cat is unable to urinate, toxins and potassium will build up in the bloodstream, leading to kidney failure and life-threatening heart rhythm issues within 24 to 48 hours. Male cats are at a significantly higher risk for complete blockages due to their narrow anatomy. If you notice your cat straining, vocalizing in the box, or licking their genitals excessively, you must seek immediate veterinary care at an emergency clinic.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Sharing a photo of the litter box contents or a brief video of your cat's posture can be incredibly helpful for a triage professional. It allows the veterinary team to differentiate between constipation and urinary straining, and helps them assess the severity of the cat's distress. A photo showing the size of any urine clumps (even if they are the size of a dime) provides vital information about whether the blockage is partial or complete.

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. If your cat is unable to pass urine, every hour counts. A total blockage can lead to permanent organ damage or death very quickly.

faqs.question: Is my cat just constipated?
faqs.answer: While it can look similar, urinary blockages are much more common and far more dangerous in cats. It is safest to assume it is a urinary issue until a vet proves otherwise.

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of a urinary blockage?
faqs.answer: Common signs include frequent trips to the box, straining, crying out while attempting to go, blood in the urine, and lethargy or hiding.
